title: Keynote SBLP: Practical MLIR for Heterogeneous Compilation - Sala Alan Turing (auditório FAU)
description: MLIR is reshaping how we build compilers by providing a multi-level, extensible infrastructure where domain-specific dialects, types, and transformations compose cleanly from high-level abstractions down to machine code. In this keynote, we share how we leverages MLIR to accelerate research and tool-building across heterogeneous systems, with a focus on real-world pipelines rather than toy examples. We'll explore three fronts. First, numeric representation with base2: a dialect supporting fixed-point and IEEE-754 floating-point types with arithmetic, enabling disciplined experimentation with precision, performance, and hardware cost. Second, data-flow computation with dfg-mlir: a Kahn Process Network (KPN) representation where processes and channels are first-class MLIR constructs, lowering to multiple backends—including OpenMP for CPU-parallel execution, an Alveo FPGA path via Olympus and Bambu HLS, and a CIRCT-based general FPGA backend slated for deprecation. Throughout, we'll highlight how structured transformations and cross-dialect optimizations deliver portability without sacrificing performance. We'll close with a forward look at Compute In/Near Memory (CIM/CNM) integrations and the dialect and lowering patterns that make such emerging architectures tractable.
